coredata.dc:title: Role of donor/acceptor of doping on nonlinear optical properties of CdS nanocomposite decorated by carbon quantum dots and reduced graphene oxide
coredata.dc:creator: Nadafan, Marzieh
Sabbaghan, Maryam
Ahmadi, Zohreh
coredata.dc:description: Cadmium sulfide (CdS), reduced graphene oxide (RGO), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), CQDs/CdS and RGO/CdS nanocomposites have been synthesized. The products have been characterized by FESEM, XRD, UV–visible spectroscopy and diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). The nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of all products are measured using Z-scan technique at 532 nm wavelength. The results reveal that the formation of structures resulting in dπ–pπ delocalized systems makes the samples a perfect choice for NLO devices. NLO properties of CQDs/CdS is attributed to the charges or energy transfer between CQDs and CdS, in the CQDs/CdS hybrid. Overall, the laser excitation of the small CdS (36 nm) in the RGO/CdS hybrid material promotes electron and/or energy transfer to the RGO component. Therefore, RGO has more effect on NLO properties of CdS than CQDs on CdS nanocomposites. This enhanced electron transfer process leads to improved NLO performances, making the hybrid material a promising candidate for various optoelectronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cadmium sulfide
Carbon dots
Reduced graphene oxide
Composite
Z-scan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Shahid Rajaee Teacher Training University under contact number 5040 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The CQDs in (a) day light and (b) under UV radiation light. The CQDs/CdS in (c) day light and (d) under UV radiation light.
XRD pattern of (a) CQDs, CdS, CQDs/CdS, and (b) GO, RGO, and RGO/CdS.
SEM images of (a) CdS, (b) CQDs/CdS, (c) GO, (d) RGO, and (e) RGO/CdS, in different magnified scales.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of all synthesized samples. (αhν)2 against hν of (b) CdS, (c) CQDs, (d) CQDs/CdS, (e) GO, (f) RGO, (g) RGO/CdS.

Normalized OA Z-scan profile of (a) CdS, (b) CQDs, (c) CQDs/CdS, (d) GO (e) RGO and (f) RGO/CdS. The solid lines represent the theoretical curves, while the spotted curves represent the experimental curves.

Normalized CA Z-scan curves of (a) CdS, (b) GO, (c) RGO, (d) RGO/CdS, (e) CQDs, and (f) CQDs/CdS. The solid lines represent the theoretical curves, while the spotted curves represent the experimental curves.
NLR index (left curves) and NLA coefficient (right curves) of all samples.
XRD results and Eg of prepared samples.
Nonlinear Optical Properties of RGO/CdS in Comparison with similar Materials.

head.ce:title: Role of donor/acceptor of doping on nonlinear optical properties of CdS nanocomposite decorated by carbon quantum dots and reduced graphene oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Shahid Rajaee Teacher Training University, Lavizan, P.O. Box 16788-15811, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Shahid Rajaee Teacher Training University, Tehran, Lavizan, P. O. Box 16788-15811, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Shahid Rajaee Teacher Training University, P.O. Box 16785-163, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Shahid Rajaee Teacher Training University, PO Box 16785-163, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cadmium sulfide (CdS), reduced graphene oxide (RGO), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), CQDs/CdS and RGO/CdS nanocomposites have been synthesized. The products have been characterized by FESEM, XRD, UV–visible spectroscopy and diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). The nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of all products are measured using Z-scan technique at 532 nm wavelength. The results reveal that the formation of structures resulting in dπ–pπ delocalized systems makes the samples a perfect choice for NLO devices. NLO properties of CQDs/CdS is attributed to the charges or energy transfer between CQDs and CdS, in the CQDs/CdS hybrid. Overall, the laser excitation of the small CdS (36 nm) in the RGO/CdS hybrid material promotes electron and/or energy transfer to the RGO component. Therefore, RGO has more effect on NLO properties of CdS than CQDs on CdS nanocomposites. This enhanced electron transfer process leads to improved NLO performances, making the hybrid material a promising candidate for various optoelectronic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional graphitic material containing hexagonal networks, reduced graphene oxide (RGO) and zero-dimensional allotrope, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have drawn increasing attention due to many applications such as electronics, energy storage, photovoltaic devices, membrane materials, resources recovery, biological engineering, biocatalysts, lightweight/strong composite materials, which are explained in the literatures [1–10].

The study of the optical properties of cadmium sulfide (CdS) got attention due to application in photonic devices, solar cells, light emitting diodes, transistors, and lasers [11–14]. The properties of CdS can be improved by changing the particle size, ability to doped or preparation of composites it with suitable compounds [15,16]. There are many different composites of CdS which exhibit linear and nonlinear optical outstanding performance in different matrix [17,18].

Graphene is a unique material that is made up of a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal pattern. These carbon atoms are bonded together in a specific way called sp2 bonding, which gives graphene its distinct electronic, mechanical, optical, and nonlinear optical (NLO) properties. Additionally, pristine graphene typically has a high defect density, which further limits its applicability in many areas. To overcome these challenges, various techniques have been developed to modify graphene and improve its processability. One common approach is functionalization, which involves introducing different functional groups or molecules onto the graphene surface. This can enhance dispersibility and prevent agglomeration by disrupting the π-π stacking interactions [19].

Non-linear optics has been a rapidly expanding scientific field in the current decades. Here, CQDs/CdS and RGO/CdS nanocomposites were synthesized by hydrothermal method. The as prepared products are characterized by absorption spectra, FESEM images, and XRD analysis. Through systematic comparative analysis, we elucidate the influence of CQDs and RGO on the optical behavior of CdS, shedding light on their potential applications in advanced photonic and optoelectronic devices. The linear and nonlinear optical properties of CdS decorated by CQDs or RGO have been studied and compared.

Graphite powder, cadmium acetate, thiourea (CH4N2S), ammonium citrate (C6H11NO7) ethylenediamine, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl, Merck), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and ethanol (C2H5OH), ascorbic acid, deionized water was also used in the synthesis of products.

During the 12-hour period, the ammonium citrate (0.2 g) and ethylenediamine (0.25 mL) dissolved in the deionized water (20 mL) undergo a reaction under high temperature (180 °C) and pressure conditions inside the Teflon-lined autoclave.

After the reaction, the reactor was allowed to cool down naturally to room temperature (r.t.). The resulting products were then collected by removing the larger particles through centrifugation at a speed of 12,000 rpm for a duration of 20 min. The collected Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were then stored at a temperature of 4 °C for subsequent characterization and usage [20]. Fig. 1(a, b) indicates the CQDs in day light and under UV radiation light.

The addition of thiourea (0.01 mol) drop by drop to the cadmium acetate (0.01 mol) solution with stirring is done to ensure proper mixing and homogeneity of the two solutions. This process helps in forming a stable microemulsion, where the cadmium acetate and thiourea molecules are dispersed evenly throughout the water solution. The experimental procedure described involves transferring a mixture into a Teflon-lined autoclave with a 50 mL capacity. The autoclave is then heated at 160 °C for a duration of 10 h. Upon completion of heating, the mixture is allowed to cool to room temperature naturally.

The process involved dissolving 0.01 mol of cadmium acetate in 30 mL of a CQD solution using magnetic stirring for 30 min. Separately, 0.01 mol of thiourea was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and added drop by drop to the above solution while stirring, resulting in the formation of a microemulsion. The microemulsion was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature. The resulting CdS nanoparticles were washed multiple times with deionized water and ethanol to remove any impurities or byproducts. Finally, the washed CdS nanoparticles were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h, resulting in the final product. The amount of Carbon to CdS is calculated about 5 % approximately. Fig. 1(c) indicates the CQDs/CdS in daylight and under UV radiation light.
Graphene oxide was prepared by the method reported in our previous article [21].
Reduced graphene oxide was prepared by the method reported in our previous article [21].

Cadmium acetate (0.01 mol) was dissolved in RGO (0.05 g in 30 mL deionized water) solution with magnetic stirring for 30 min. 0.01 mol of thiourea was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and added drop by drop to the above solution while stirring, resulting in the formation of a microemulsion. The microemulsion was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature. The resulting CdS nanoparticles were washed multiple times with deionized water and ethanol to remove any impurities or byproducts. Finally, the washed CdS nanoparticles were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The amount of carbon to CdS is calculated to be about 5 % approximately.

The XRD patterns of the GO, RGO and RGO/CdS are shown in Fig. 2b. The XRD patterns of GO exhibit a slightly broad peak at 9.14° and 43.1° matching with the refection from the (002) and (111) plane respectively [21,23]. In the case of XRD pattern of RGO a broad peak at 23.9° and a small peak at 43.1° due to (002) and (111) planes, respectively were appeared [21]. These peaks have not been observed in XRD pattern of RGO/CdS due to less content of RGO in nanocomposite. The XRD pattern of RGO/CdS shows high crystallinity of CdS component (Table 1 ).

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of the products. The SEM of CdS Show cauliflower morphology (Fig. 3a). Induction of CQD on CdS did not structure change of CdS in CQDs/CdS nanocomposite, as also seen in XRD analysis (Fig. 3b). As it was reported in our earlier article, SEM of GO shows well-exfoliated and smooth sheets without crumples which are produced with modified Hummer's method. Also, SEM of RGO revealed surface consists of compressed flakes of graphene (Fig. 3c, d) [20]. Furthermore, the morphology of CdS in GO/CdS nanocomposite did not change in comparison with the morphology of CdS (Fig. 3b, e).

Fig. 4(a) illustrates the optical absorption spectra of all synthesized samples. The absorption peak of CQDs/CdS shows the red shift in comparison with CQDs. This trend observed in RGO/CdS that it also has red shift in comparison with GO and RGO. The exhibit red-shifted linear absorption with respect to monomers usually due to interactions between RGO or CQDs and CdS, such as energy transfer or electron transfer between them. According to related studies, the significant red shift of CQDs/CdS and RGO/CdS are expectable improvement of optical properties in comparison with pure CQDs and RGO [24–26]. Considering Tauc-plot relation, the estimated band gap of CdS, CQDs, CQDs/CdS, GO, RGO, and RGO/CdS are 2.55, 3.2, 3.15, 2.95, 2.55, and 2.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b–g) [25,26]. The band gap reduction observed in CQDs/CdS and RGO/CdS because of interfacial interaction between CdS and CQDs or CdS and GO substrate [24].

The Z-scan method, which was introduced by Sheikh Bahaee in 1990, is a useful and adaptable technique for determining both the sign and magnitude of NLO coefficients. It involves distorting a Gaussian beam by moving a sample along its propagation path, thereby altering the wavefront phase through self-focusing or self-defocusing (positive or negative) at the beam focus. This method provides a valuable assessment of the NLO treatment of materials [27,40].

In previous scientific investigations [23,27], researchers proposed a cool Z-scan setup for a continuous-wave (CW) laser. In this particular study, we used a super fancy horizontally polarized Gaussian CW Nd:YAG laser, which emitted a beautiful green light at 532 nm, with a power of 40 mW. we directed and concentrated this laser beam through a lens with a focal length of 19 cm. during our scanning process, we moved the samples at regular intervals of 1 mm.

To find out the NLR index and NLA coefficient, we took a fascinating approach. We measured the transmitted beam's amplitude and phase using two methods: one with a small aperture in front of the detector (called CA), and another one without any aperture (called OA). These measurements were done while positioning the sample at various distances in the z-direction, relative to the focal plane. All samples underwent investigation for this particular purpose.

OA and CA of Z-scan technique detected the third order NLO properties of the samples that are referred to nonlinear absorption, NLA and nonlinear refraction, NLR. Two detectors can record the intensities of the transmitted or the input laser power. The proposed Z-scan experimental setup is shown by details in various studies as shown in Fig. 5 [17,18]. NLA coefficient, β evaluated from the OA Z-scan, calculated by (Eq. 1):
(1)
β =
2
2
1 − T
z
I 0
L eff

z 2
z 0 2
in
which
L eff

1 − exp
− αL
/ α
z 0
=
πω 0 2
/ λ
α = − ln
− P /
P 0
/ L
where L
eff , I 0, α, z 0, P 0 and P are the effective thickness, the intensity of laser, the linear absorption, the Rayleigh length, the input and the output power, respectively. The normalized transmittance at z = 0 is represented by the symbol T
z
. As shown in Fig. 6 , the beam energy density was minimum at the focus, which resulted in the two-photon absorption (TPA) effect at various z positions. The calculation of the NLA coefficient (β) was based on the OA experimental data. The empirical and theoretical findings of the NLA were confirmed using Eqs. 2 and 3.
(2)
T norm
z
=
∑
m = 0
∞

z 0
m
m + 1
3 / 2
(3)
q 0
z t
=
β
I 0
L eff
/
1 +
z 2
/
z 2
0

The Z-scan data using CA technique displayed a peak-valley or valley-peak configurations for transmittance, indicating negative or positive NLR indices and the self-defocusing or self-focusing pattern of the specimens, respectively. In CdS, GO, RGO, and CQDs, the CA Z-scan curves implies the positive nonlinear refraction properties, whereas RGO/CdS, and CQDs/CdS curves show the negative nonlinear refraction properties. Eq. 4 shows that the difference between peak and valley transmittance, Δ
T
P − V
is associated with an on-axis shift of the phase Δ
φ 0
:
(4)
Δ
T
P − V
= 0.406
1 − S
0.25
Δ
φ 0
Δ
φ 0
=
2 π / λ
n 2
I 0
L eff

The symbol S = 0.23–0.29, while n2 represents the NLR index. Also, the experimental CA values can thus be fitted with the following equation [21,22]:
(5)
T norm
= 1 −
4 x Δ
φ 0
1 +
x 2
9 +
x 2
where
x =
z
z 0

The nonlinear optical susceptibility of samples was calculated by the following equations [27]:
(6)
Re
χ
3
esu
=
10
− 4
π
ε 0
c 2
n 0 2
n 2
cm 2
W
(7)
Im
χ
3
esu
=
10
− 2
4
π 2
ε 0
c 2
n 0 2
λβ
cm W

The refractive index of CdS, CQDs, and GO are 2.66, 1.23, and 2.1, respectively. In the context of light-matter interaction, susceptibility refers to the ability of a material to respond to an applied electric field. It quantifies the polarization of the material, which is the induced separation of positive and negative charges within the material when exposed to an electric field (Fig. 7 ).

The order of NLR index and NLA coefficient of samples were
10
− 8
cm 2
/ W
and
10
− 4
cm / W
, respectively, as shown in Table 2 . The NLO procedures of all samples are presented in Fig. 8 . It indicates the NLR index and NLA coefficient of CdS is lower and RGO/CdS is higher than other samples.

Recently, the NLO action of graphene and graphene-based hybrids has garnered significant attention in recent years. Researchers have made increasing efforts to explore their potential applications in the field of nonlinear optics [28–31]. For instance, a study reported enhanced two-photon absorption (2PA) properties of GO-Ag nanocomposites when compared to those of GO. The nanocomposites exhibited increased 2PA under the excitation of a nanosecond pulsed visible laser radiation with a wavelength of 532 nm. The extracted value of 2PA for GO-Ag was found to be 45.4 cm GW−1 [28].

Based on the information provided, it can be inferred that the NLO properties of CdS loaded with graphene sheets vary due to their interactions. The NLA of CdS trimmed RGO was found to be significantly higher than other graphene composites such as GO-Ag [28]. In graphene sheets on a substrate, the contribution to NLR mainly comes from π-electrons, and the thermal effect can be considered insignificant because graphene has an exceptionally high thermal diffusion coefficient. However, when in dispersed solution states, considerations must be given to factors such as π-electrons, thermal effects from solution dispersions, reorientation, and alignment of graphene sheets. Typically, nonlinearity can arise from a variety of factors, including electronic, molecular, electrostrictive, or thermal effects. These various mechanisms contribute to the phenomenon of nonlinearity. In this case, the nonlinearity primarily originates from thermal influences, as the relaxation time spans milliseconds and the difference between peaks and valleys exceeds 1.7 ZR.

Recently, there were reports on NLO properties of GO, CQDs, CdS, GO/CQDs but there are no reports on NLO properties of RGO/CdS and comparison between CQDs/CdS and RGO/CdS composites [32–36]. According to Vijesh et al. papers, the nonlinearity of the carbon dots (CDs)-GO is due to the reorientation of the GO sheets in the presence of electromagnetic field of the laser and 2PA [37]. Li et al. reported the NLO treatment of GO/CdS composites, in which, the increasing number of CdS nanoparticles on graphene sheets strengthens the synergistic effects between the two components, resulting in a composite material with improved nonlinear properties and potential for various technological applications [38].

Previous research has shown that the morphology and structure of specimens have a significant impact on their NLO properties [21]. The transfer of electrons and π-electron delocalization in the samples can lead to improved nonlinearity responses [23]. Surendran et al. synthesized CQDs by orange peel and recorded the NLO properties of CQDs [33]. They reported the self-defocusing and RSA for NLR and NLA coefficient of CQDs in 532 nm wavelength [33]. The formation of structures resulting in dπ–pπ delocalized systems makes the samples a perfect choice for NLO devices. NLO properties of CQDs/CdS is attributed to the charges or energy transfer between CQDs and CdS, in the CQDs/CdS hybrid [39].

Additionally, the incorporation of graphene into CdS nanoparticles can also promote two-photon absorption (2PA), which refers to the simultaneous absorption of two photons in a single event. Graphene has a unique electronic band structure that enables efficient 2PA, resulting in improved nonlinear optical properties in the composite material. Overall, the combination of graphene and CdS nanoparticles results in a synergistic effect, where the nonlinearity of CdS is enhanced by the presence of graphene and the 2PA capability of graphene. This leads to a significant improvement in the third-order nonlinear optical properties of the composite material [38]. The enhanced NLO (nonlinear optical) performances of the RGO/CdS hybrid can be explained by a process involving laser-induced electron and/or energy transfer from the ultra-small CdS QDs (electron donor) to the RGO (electron acceptor). This transfer is facilitated by the highly delocalized conjugated structure and superior electrical conductivity of the RGO. The RGO efficiently facilitates the transfer of photo-generated free carriers, preventing charge recombination and resulting in the creation of a charge-separated excited state. This charge separation contributes to the enhanced NLO response observed in the RGO/CdS hybrid system [40].

Table 3 in the paper compares the NLO properties of the present samples with previous reports on similar materials. The comparison reveals that the present samples exhibit better nonlinear properties than the others. This implies that the RGO/CdS composite has a higher third order nonlinearity. The third order nonlinearity refers to the ability of a material to exhibit nonlinear effects when it is subjected to high-intensity light. This property is crucial for applications such as optical switching and super continuum generation in telecommunication.

The optoelectronic properties of the CdS composite through preparing different composite of RGO and CQDs could be changed. Findings depicted that RGO can enhance magnitude of the linear and nonlinear optical properties of CdS. NLO properties of CQDs/CdS is attributed to the charges or energy transfer between CQDs and CdS, in the CQDs/CdS hybrid. The improved NLO characteristics of the RGO/CdS hybrid can be elucidated by the phenomenon where lasers facilitate the transfer of electrons and/or energy from the electron donor. Furthermore, improving NLO characteristics of CdS with RGO nanocomposites in comparison with CQDs nanocomposites causes a promising helpful candidate in NLO devices.

Marzieh Nadafan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Maryam Sabbaghan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Zohreh Ahmadi: Project administration, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Shahid Rajaee Teacher Training University under contact number 5040.
